did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Modified Swin Transformer (MST), a deep learning architecture, to classify dermoscopic skin lesion images for melanoma detection. The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC-2017 dataset, achieving a testing accuracy of 74.67%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a Modified Swin Transformer (MST) for melanoma detection. It details the methodology, dataset (ISIC-2017), model architecture, and experimental results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) comparing the proposed model against baselines like ResNet-101, Swin Transformer, and ViT. Although published in a conference proceedings (ECCE 2025), it reports new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Modified Swin Transformer Architecture for Accurate Melanoma Detection
journal: 2025 International Conference on Electrical, Computer and Communication Engineering (ECCE)
brief_summary: This study proposes a Modified Swin Transformer (MST) architecture for accurate melanoma detection by integrating linear layers from ResNet with the Swin Transformer feature extractor. The model was evaluated on the ISIC-2017 dermoscopic skin lesion dataset, which included approximately 2,000 training, 150 validation, and 600 test images across melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis classes. The performance of the MST was compared against state-of-the-art baseline models, including ResNet-101, Swin Transformer, and Vision Transformer (ViT). Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed MST achieved superior performance, attaining a testing accuracy of 74.67% along with improved precision, recall, and F1-score metrics. The findings indicate that the MST effectively handles class imbalance and complex image features, outperforming the compared architectures in melanoma classification tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for detection; malignant skin cancer. The study aims to distinguish melanoma from benign lesions. In the ISIC-2017 dataset, there were 374 melanoma images in the training set and 64 in the test set.

Benign skin lesion class. Included in the ISIC-2017 dataset as a non-melanoma class. There were 1,626 non-melanoma images in the training set (comprising nevus and seborrheic keratosis) and 432 nevus images in the test set.
Benign skin lesion class (non-cancerous skin growths). Included in the ISIC-2017 dataset. There were 102 seborrheic keratosis images in the test set.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs a three-class classification task to distinguish between malignant melanoma and two benign lesion classes (nevus and seborrheic keratosis) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC-2017 dataset. The model outputs class predictions evaluated via accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2017
datasets_used.description: The ISIC-2017 skin lesion dataset, a public benchmark for melanoma detection hosted by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions categorized into three classes.

use.validation: Used for fine-tuning hyperparameters and monitoring model performance during training (150 images).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation and benchmarking diagnostic accuracy (600 images).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper compares model performance using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and balanced accuracy without statistical significance tests.

ISIC-2017.notes: The Modified Swin Transformer (MST) achieved the highest testing accuracy (74.67%) and balanced accuracy (63.05%) among all models. The ViT achieved 70.83% testing accuracy. ResNet-101 and Swin Transformer had the lowest testing accuracies at 65.83% and 65.50% respectively. The MST showed improved performance in predicting Melanoma cases compared to other models.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained Swin Transformer Tiny (Swin-T) feature extractor, pre-trained on ImageNet dataset. Initial layers of the Swin Transformer were frozen during training.

architecture_details.modifications: Integration of linear layers from ResNet and custom classification head. Added three linear layers and two dropout layers to the Swin Transformer.
architecture_details.normalization: Layer normalization applied to embedded features

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (learning rate scheduler mentioned)
training_details.hardware: Linux server with four NVIDIA A6000 GPUs and an Xeon processor
training_details.transfer_learning: Initial layers frozen; final layers trained. Majority of layers frozen due to ImageNet pre-training.
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (Testing Loss values provided in results)

classification_or_output_head.structure: FC(512) -> ReLU -> Dropout(0.5) -> FC(256) -> Dropout(0.3) -> FC(3)

preprocessing_techniques: Patch extraction: images divided into non-overlapping 4x4 patches
Layer normalization applied to embedded features
conclusions: The proposed Modified Swin Transformer (MST) architecture, which integrates linear layers from ResNet with the Swin Transformer feature extractor, achieves superior performance in melanoma detection compared to ResNet-101, Swin Transformer, and Vision Transformer (ViT).
The MST model attained the highest testing accuracy of 74.67% on the ISIC-2017 dataset, along with improved precision, recall, and F1-score metrics.
The MST demonstrated a balanced accuracy of 63.05%, indicating it handled class imbalance reasonably well compared to other architectures.
The proposed model offers a reliable solution for melanoma classification tasks, particularly in scenarios involving imbalanced datasets or complex image features.
Future work will focus on improving accuracy through high-precision segmentation techniques, advanced optimization algorithms, and validation on more diverse datasets.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single public dataset (ISIC-2017) with no external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability.

The testing accuracy of 74.67% is modest for a critical diagnostic task, and the balanced accuracy of 63.05% indicates significant difficulty in handling class imbalance, particularly for the minority Melanoma class.
The model exhibits high false-negative rates for Melanoma detection: only 64.1% of Melanoma cases were correctly identified in the test set, which poses a serious clinical safety risk.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to validate the superiority of the proposed MST over baseline models.

Data leakage risks are present as the paper does not explicitly confirm that image-level splitting was used to prevent patient overlap between training and test sets, a common issue in dermatological datasets.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which may contribute to the model's limited performance on a relatively small dataset (2,000 training images).
The hardware setup (four NVIDIA A6000 GPUs) suggests high computational costs, potentially hindering deployment in resource-constrained clinical settings.
The confusion matrix reveals poor performance on Seborrheic Keratosis (56.7% accuracy), indicating the model struggles to distinguish this benign lesion from melanoma compared to nevi.
